nativeRange.habitat: Selectively bred / hobby cultivar of the Bucephalandra genus, which is endemic to the island of Borneo, where wild populations grow as rheophytes on rocks and submerged wood along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood or other porous hardscape for the rhizome to attach to
open space around the attachment point so the large leaves can extend without being crowded
low-to-moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
stable, established tank — generally advised against introducing into newly cycled systems

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp, which graze biofilm on the broad leaves
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern, mosses and other Bucephalandra cultivars
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers such as large cichlids that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters where buce melt is more likely

summary: Premium 'rare' Bucephalandra cultivar prized for its unusually large, dramatically rippled leaves. Care is the same as the genus — attach the rhizome to hardscape and never bury it — but Godzilla is commonly considered one of the largest cultivars in the trade and is generally advised to be given open space around its attachment point.
careNotes: Bucephalandra 'Godzilla' is a hobby cultivar of the Borneo-endemic Bucephalandra genus (family Araceae); like all bucephalandras it is grown as an epiphytic rheophyte attached to hardscape, never buried, and is treated similarly to Anubias. Care requirements are the same as the parent genus entry (fw-plant-016): water temperature is generally advised in the 21-28 °C range, pH around 6.0-7.5, low to medium light, slow growth, optional CO2 (which produces noticeably better coloration), and water-column fertilization at low levels (sources frequently note that stronger lighting and fertilizer enhance coloration). Godzilla is documented in the hobby as one of the largest Bucephalandra cultivars in trade, with long, broad leaves that develop a distinctive ruffled or wavy edge. Sources differ on exactly how large it gets — Glass Aqua reports 3-5"+ (≈8-13 cm) in height with long ruffled leaves; Flip Aquatics describes it as 'medium in size' with longer leaves than typical buces; Modern Aquarium notes 'bold, wavy leaves' and a tendency to form dense clusters; other trade descriptions cite individual leaves reaching 6-10 cm or more. New leaves are commonly reported to emerge reddish to brown and darken/green with maturity, sometimes with an iridescent sheen and pinkish-orange stems. Because the leaves are unusually large for the genus, multiple sources specifically recommend giving Godzilla open space around the attachment point — leaves grown in tight quarters between other plants tend to come in smaller and less impressive. Like other bucephalandras, it is prone to 'buce melt' when first transitioning from emersed nursery growth to submersed conditions or after sudden parameter changes; recovery from a firm rhizome is generally reported over the following weeks. Propagate by rhizome division, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach to a new piece of hardscape. The intended Buceplant secondary URL was unavailable at research time (404); Flip Aquatics and Glass Aqua were used as alternative confirming secondaries.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is by rhizome division: cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean scissors, leaving each piece with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the division to a separate piece of hardscape using super glue gel, sewing thread or fishing line. Godzilla is reported to also produce lateral side shoots from the rhizome that can be detached once they have rooted. Growth is slow, so a freshly divided plant typically needs several weeks to begin putting out new leaves.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirms origin (fast-flowing rivers of Borneo), temperature range 70-82°F (≈21-28 °C), low-to-moderate lighting, slow growth, midground-to-background placement, propagation by rhizome division and side shoots, requirement to attach to hardscape and never bury the rhizome, and characteristic bold, wavy leaves with deep green to bluish-purple coloration.

additional.name: Flip Aquatics
additional.notes: Secondary source (substituted after the intended Buceplant URL returned 404). Cross-checked: Borneo origin, beginner care level, low-to-medium lighting (high light enhances coloration), optional CO2, mid-to-background placement, propagation by rhizome cutting, attachment to driftwood/rock, and the cultivar-specific traits of longer leaves, ruffling, and brown/red submersed leaf coloration.

additional.name: Glass Aqua
additional.notes: Additional secondary used for sizing. Reports plant height of 3-5"+ (≈8-13 cm) with long, ruffled-edged leaves and dark green foliage; confirms easy care, low-to-medium light, slow growth, midground placement, optional CO2, propagation by division and attachment to hardscape, do-not-bury-rhizome rule, and susceptibility to algae on the slow-growing leaves.

additional.name: Bucephalandra (genus parent entry fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Cross-referenced shared genus husbandry (water parameters 21-28 °C / pH 6-8, low-to-medium lighting, optional CO2, slow growth, epiphytic placement, do-not-bury-rhizome rule, buce melt during emersed-to-submersed transition, propagation by rhizome division). The genus entry already documents Godzilla as one of the taller cultivars in the 15-25 cm range; this cultivar entry inherits that genus baseline.
